The river has breached the banks and drowned the winter wheat in silt.
If the lord does not waive the tithe, my children will starve before spring.
I must carry the goats to the high ridge before the mud takes them.
My cottage roof has collapsed under the rain, and I have no timber left.
We spent all season tilling this soil only for the wind to strip it.
The sky be turning a sickly shade of green and I fear for the winter wheat.
Lord Alistair will surely blame us poor souls if the livestock perish from this omen.
I must hurry the goats back to the fold before the heavens crack wide open.
May the saints preserve us, for the stars be dancing in a way I ne'er seen.
If the rain turns to fire, there be no shelter but the dirt beneath my feet.
That stone marker has stood since my grandfather's time, and I'll not see it moved a finger's width.
If you take that strip of loam, my youngest will have no place to plant the winter rye.
The Lord's bailiff will have my head if the tithe is short because you've stolen my grazing land.
I have bled into this soil for twenty years, and I know exactly where the creek bends.
You may have the fancy papers, but the dirt knows who has worked it since the Great Frost.
Lord mercy, what be this shimmering hole in the air right where my turnips grow?
I dare not step near that glow, for the bailiff will have my head if I vanish.
Does this strange light mean the soil be cursed or some spirit come for the harvest?
I’ve naught but this rusted hoe to defend me against whatever dwells inside that swirling mist.
If the master finds I've been idling by this magic door, I'll be whipped for a day.
My boots are soaked through and these brambles keep tearing at my rough wool tunic.
I must find a way through these thickets before the sun sets or I'll freeze.
If I can just find the old deer trail, I might make it home for supper.
This forest is too thick for a cart, so I'll have to carry the sacks.
I hope the lord doesn't punish me for being late with the harvest tithes tonight.
The soil is dry and the wind has stopped, yet no voice calls from the manor.
I have leaned upon my hoe for hours, listening for a word that never comes.
The village square is empty and the heavy quiet makes my heart beat fast.
My hands are stained with earth and my throat is parched from this long stillness.
I wonder if the lord has forgotten us all in this deep and hollow silence.
Please, good sir, I have no coin to give, only the meager grain in my sack.
I beg of you to spare my life, for my children have no one else to plow the fields.
I will give you everything I own if you only let me return to my cottage tonight.
Merciful lord, I meant no offense and I shall bow my head in total submission.
My only crime was being in the way, so I pray you show a poor soul some pity.
This iron pot is the only thing I own that keeps the pottage warm.
I keep my father's rusted sickle hidden well away from the bailiff's prying eyes.
This woolen shawl is thin and frayed, but it keeps the winter chill off.
I have saved three copper pennies in a hole beneath the dirt floor.
My only pride is this sturdy pair of boots that keep the mud out.
This bag of seed must last until the first rains come to the valley.
I cannot let the lord's bailiff see how little grain is left in the cellar.
If the winter is harsh, my children will have nothing but watery porridge to eat.
We must stretch these few scraps of leather to patch all the boots before snow.
I will carve the smallest portions tonight so that we might survive another week.
